inline_review:
commit 214d23b786dc7d60d46006f05bb5987a1a2752c1
Author: Leno Hou <lenohou@gmail.com>

mm/mglru: fix cgroup OOM during MGLRU state switching

This patch introduces an intermediate lru_switch state to bridge the
transition when MGLRU is being enabled or disabled. It forces the
reclaimer to attempt both MGLRU and traditional reclaim paths sequentially
to prevent out of memory kills caused by reclaim vacuums.

> Changes
> =======
> v5:
>  - Rename lru_gen_draining to lru_gen_switching; lru_drain_core to
>    lru_switch
>  - Add more documentation for folio_referenced_one
>  - Keep folio_check_references unchanged

This isn't a bug, but the commit message indicates folio_check_references
was kept unchanged, while the provided diff modifies it.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c
> @@ -886,7 +886,7 @@ static enum folio_references folio_check_references(struct folio *folio,
>  	if (referenced_ptes == -1)
>  		return FOLIOREF_KEEP;
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (lru_gen_enabled() && !lru_gen_switching()) {
>  		if (!referenced_ptes)
>  			return FOLIOREF_RECLAIM;

Does this change bypass MGLRU-specific workingset tracking?

When lru_gen_switching() is true, MGLRU folios being reclaimed bypass the
MGLRU-specific logic and fall through to the traditional LRU logic. The
traditional logic calls folio_test_clear_referenced(), which forcefully
clears the PG_referenced bit.

Since MGLRU uses PG_referenced as part of its tier tracking flags
(LRU_REFS_FLAGS), could this corrupt the tier state of MGLRU folios being
reclaimed and bypass the workingset tracking updates in lru_gen_set_refs()?

[ ... ]

> @@ -6182,10 +6191,13 @@ static void shrink_node(pg_data_t *pgdat, struct scan_control *sc)
>  	struct lruvec *target_lruvec;
>  	bool reclaimable = false;
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled() && root_reclaim(sc)) {
> +	if ((lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_switching()) && root_reclaim(sc)) {
>  		memset(&sc->nr, 0, sizeof(sc->nr));
>  		lru_gen_shrink_node(pgdat, sc);
> -		return;
> +
> +		if (!lru_gen_switching())
> +			return;
> +
>  	}
>  
>  	target_lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(sc->target_mem_cgroup, pgdat);

If lru_gen_switching() is true, execution falls through to the traditional
shrink_node_memcgs() path below.

In shrink_node_memcgs(), the iteration loop over memcgs checks the global
reclaim target termination condition (sc->nr_reclaimed >= sc->nr_to_reclaim)
only at the end of the loop block.

If MGLRU has already successfully reclaimed the target number of pages in
lru_gen_shrink_node(), will this fallthrough cause an unnecessary and full
scale reclaim on the first memcg in the iteration before the loop terminates?

[ ... ]

> @@ -6845,9 +6857,12 @@ static void kswapd_age_node(struct pglist_data *pgdat, struct scan_control *sc)
>  	struct mem_cgroup *memcg;
>  	struct lruvec *lruvec;
>  
> -	if (lru_gen_enabled()) {
> +	if (lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_switching()) {
>  		lru_gen_age_node(pgdat, sc);
> -		return;
> +
> +		if (!lru_gen_switching())
> +			return;
> +
>  	}
>  
>  	lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(NULL, pgdat);

Could this force spurious out of memory kills during state transitions if
lru_gen_min_ttl is configured?

When MGLRU is being disabled, lru_gen_change_state() actively drains the
MGLRU lists. Concurrently, if lru_gen_min_ttl is greater than 0,
lru_gen_age_node() initializes its reclaimable flag to false and checks
lruvec_is_reclaimable().

Since the lists are being drained and are empty, lruvec_is_sizable()
evaluates to false for all lruvecs.

This leaves the reclaimable flag as false and unconditionally triggers
out_of_memory() inside lru_gen_age_node(), which seems to contradict the
patch's purpose of avoiding out of memory kills.
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Spurious OOM kills triggered during MGLRU state transition when `min_ttl` is configured.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"In `kswapd_age_node()`, the patch forces `lru_gen_age_node(pgdat, sc)` to execute during state transitions via the `|| lru_gen_switching()` condition. When MGLRU is being disabled, `lru_gen_change_state()` actively drains MGLRU lists. Concurrently, if `lru_gen_min_ttl > 0`, `lru_gen_age_node()` initializes `reclaimable = false`. It then checks `lruvec_is_reclaimable()`, which evaluates MGLRU list sizes via `lruvec_is_sizable()`. Since the lists are being drained and are empty, it returns false for all lruvecs. This leaves `reclaimable` as false and unconditionally triggers `out_of_memory(&oc)`. This causes spurious OOM kills, directly contradicting the patch's purpose."},{"problem":"MGLRU tier state corruption and missing workingset tracking during state transitions.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"Despite the changelog claiming to keep `folio_check_references` unchanged, the patch alters it to `if (lru_gen_enabled() && !lru_gen_switching())`. When `lru_gen_switching()` is true, MGLRU folios being reclaimed bypass the MGLRU-specific logic and fall through to traditional LRU logic. The traditional logic calls `folio_test_clear_referenced(folio)`, which forcefully clears the `PG_referenced` bit. Since MGLRU relies on `PG_referenced` as part of its tier tracking flags (`LRU_REFS_FLAGS`), this clears and corrupts the tier state of MGLRU folios, while also bypassing the workingset tracking updates in `lru_gen_set_refs()`."},{"problem":"Unfair and excessive double reclaim from the first memcg during state transitions.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"In `shrink_node()`, if a state transition is active, the execution falls through from `lru_gen_shrink_node()` to the traditional `shrink_node_memcgs()` path. `shrink_node_memcgs()` operates with a `do-while` loop that checks the global reclaim target termination condition (`if (partial && sc->nr_reclaimed >= sc->nr_to_reclaim)`) only at the *end* of the loop block. If MGLRU has already successfully reclaimed the target number of pages, this check will succeed, but only after `shrink_lruvec()` and `shrink_slab()` have unconditionally executed for the first `memcg` in the iteration. This inflicts an unnecessary and full scale reclaim on the first memcg, unfairly penalizing it."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 214d23b786dc7d60d46006f05bb5987a1a2752c1
